macro_segments.title: Introduction to Hasagat Gvul: The Doctrine of Encroaching on Business Territory

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on Hasagat Gvul as a policy question between protectionism and free market
Rav Huna's ruling: prohibition on competing mills in the same mavoi
Debate between Chachamim and R' Yehuda regarding unfair business practices with candy and nuts

macro_segments.title: Property Rights Framework: David's Intervention on Hasagat Gvul as Theft

micro_segments.title: David's explanation: Hasagat Gvul based on property rights; customers as already-owned business
Connection to fishnet case and the measure of fish distance (kimlo ritzas hadag)

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Opening Competing Stores: The Basic Permission and Its Limits

micro_segments.title: Opening a store or bathhouse next to a competitor's: the permission to do what you want in your property
Distinction between mavoi (alleyway) rules and neighbor conflicts; zoning vs. individual property
Economic analysis: competition concentrating businesses improves traffic and outcomes for all

macro_segments.title: Dispute Over What Can Stop Competing Businesses: Tanna'im Debate on Neighbor Rights

micro_segments.title: Tannaim debate: Chachamim vs Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel on stopping neighbor's competing business
Rav Huna bar Rav Yehoshua clarifies: obvious that you can stop outsiders from different towns
Condition: if both pay same tax (karga), outsider is not truly foreign; cannot be stopped from public places
Question: can someone from one mavoi prevent business from someone in another mavoi?

macro_segments.title: Educational and Social Overrides to Business Protection Rules

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef: even Rav Huna agrees you can open competing schools despite business restrictions
Principle of Kinat Sofrim Tarbe Chochma: competition in Torah improves wisdom
Itinerant merchants (rochlin) permitted to sell fragrances and jewelry by Ezra's takkanah for women's benefit
Rochlin can only pass through (le'ahaduri), not establish (le'akbui); exception for talmidei chachamim
Rava allows R' Yoshiya and R' Ovadia to establish shops despite outside status due to Torah learning needs

macro_segments.title: Application to Market Days and Temporary Sellers: Ravina's Ruling on Basket Sellers

micro_segments.title: Case of outside wicker basket sellers (dikulei) challenged by townspeople; Ravina permits on market day
Restriction: outside sellers may only sell on market day (yoma d'shuka) in the marketplace, not door-to-door
Tosafot application: outside moneylenders can lend to non-local visitors and non-Jews on market day only

macro_segments.title: Case of Outside Wool Merchants: Rav Kahana's Balanced Ruling on Debt Collection and Livelihood

micro_segments.title: Outside wool sellers challenged by town; Rav Kahana initially permits town to prevent them
Merchants claim they have outstanding debts (ashrai) to collect in the city; Rav Kahana's compromise
Ruling: merchants may sell only what they need to earn minimal living until debts are collected

macro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's Figs and the Testing of Talmidei Chachamim: Competitive Dynamics Among Scholars

micro_segments.title: Rav Dimi from Nehardea brings dried figs; Resh Galuta instructs Rava to test if he is a talmid chacham
Rav Adda bar Ahava's impossible question about elephant swallowing Egyptian basket; Rav Dimi cannot answer
Rav Adda kicks Rav Dimi and declares gap between himself and Rava; refuses him monopoly on figs
Rav Dimi's figs spoil; he complains to Rav Yosef, who pronounces a curse against Rav Adda

macro_segments.title: Rav Adda bar Ahava's Death: Multiple Scholars Taking Blame for a Curse's Effect

micro_segments.title: Rav Adda dies shortly after Rav Yosef's curse; multiple rabbis claim responsibility for his death
Rav Yosef: I cursed him because he poached my students with promises of better meat
Rav Dimi: I am to blame because my bringing figs caused the entire event
Abaye: I am to blame because he tried to steal my students; I should have rebuked him
Rava: I am to blame because he showed disrespect to my attendant over meat at the butcher
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak: I am to blame; he failed to help me prepare, I wished him dead
Rav Nachman explicitly says 'I am waiting for his coffin'; dies immediately upon announcement

macro_segments.title: Wall Construction Rules: Distance Requirements and Passageway Concerns

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: if your wall is near neighbor's wall, do not build unless four amot away
Windows on boundary wall: four amot distance required both vertically and horizontally for privacy
Rav Yehuda's reading: applies when wanting to build new wall, not describing existing situation
Rava's alternative: original wall was four amot away, fell, now cannot rebuild closer than four amot
Reason (Mai Ta'ama): trampling foottraffic hardens the ground supporting the wall; passage benefit to neighbor

Mavoi (alleyway/semi-public courtyard)

Rav Huna (strict position on business protection)
Rav Yosef (allows schools exception)
Rava (allows talmidei chachamim special permissions)
Ravina (permits outside sellers on market day)
Rav Kahana (balances town rights with merchant needs)
Rav Dimi (merchant with figs; undergoes testing)
Rav Adda bar Ahava (tests Rav Dimi; dies after rejection)
Ashrai (credit/debt owed by customers)
Rav Yosef's curse on Rav Adda for poaching students
Abaye taking blame for not rebuking Rav Adda
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak waiting for Rav Adda's coffin

Trampling foottraffic hardening ground (mechusa d'hacha ma'ale lehacha)
